And so you can imagine a situation where border officers -- and I'm not suggesting this as part -- as part of an -- an overarching policy -- but where individual border officers do decide to just parole returning LPRs in large numbers. They don't need to satisfy any standard under the government's view other than whatever satisfies the officer. And then that can just -- that can just continue indefinitely while the government pursues a fishing expedition until --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, Mr. Dvoretzky, is -- is -- does your argument depend on our acceptance of this conspiracy theory?

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: No. It -- it depends on -- it's simply the logical consequence of the government's argument that if there is no meaningful check on the ability to parole LPRs, then that power might be used. And it might lead to a situation where, again, LPRs are paroled just based on a mere suspicion, as your own hypothetical suggested to Mr. Joshi early on in the argument, you look kind of fishy to me. That can then go on for a long time.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Yeah, but that's not what we have here. We have a criminal charge. And the -- the immigration officer didn't make that up. The charge was leveled by the State of New Jersey. I guess the State of New Jersey is in on this conspiracy?

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: Again, I don't think it's a conspiracy, but what I do think is that the charge is not -- a charge does not show that the person has actually committed the offense, that -- that -- that there's still a -- a presumption of innocence at that point. And the charge doesn't show that he's committed the offense.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, it doesn't say -- 5 doesn't say was convicted. How about Romanette (iii), has engaged in illegal activity after having departed the United States? Same rule there?

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: I think it would be the same rule. The question is, is there some evidence before the officer or does the LPR admit to something in an interview? Again, people do admit to things in interviews that perhaps they shouldn't, and -- and that's how that would be applied. But, again, to -- to the --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Let's say an LP -- I'm sorry. Go ahead.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: No, no, please.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: An -- an LPR leaves the United States, goes to France, flies back to the United States. While the plane is in the air, the federal government receives an urgent message from the French police, he shot somebody while he was here just before he got on the plane, but he hasn't been convicted, and in that instance, he hasn't even been charged. Would he fall -- would that person fall within Romanette (iii), has engaged in illegal activity after having departed the United States?

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: I -- I don't think so. I think, in that situation, we -- we don't know that, but, again, all of this goes to the question of what is the level of proof required at the border. That question has not been briefed. These are complicated questions that are not directly answered by the statute. They involve background principles. They might involve policy considerations. None of that has been briefed. And, in fact, the government expressly disclaimed that in the cert briefing and didn't present it to the Second Circuit either.

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: And how --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Did I understand your -- your answer to Justice Alito's question was no, he could not be detained?

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: If all we have is --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: All you have is a phone call from the French police saying we think he just shot -- shot somebody, he comes to the border, and the -- the officer at the border cannot detain him?

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: I think that would be no more than the equivalent of a charge, which doesn't prove that he actually engaged in the conduct, but, again, whether or not that would be sufficient, I think the point is there is some burden at the border to -- to show that one of these exceptions was satisfied, and the --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: No, no, I know your -- I know your general rule. It just seems to me to be pretty bizarre to say that in that situation they couldn't even be detained.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: Again, perhaps he -- perhaps he could be detained in that situation. My point is none of this has been fleshed out. These are questions that the government has actually disclaimed.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: So they -- they could say, look, all I have is a call from the -- the -- the police in France that you just killed somebody, we need to flesh that out a little bit?

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: Well, it -- it -- what it might be is probable cause for -- for a non-immigration arrest in that situation. You could imagine that, that it satisfies probable cause. But whether probable cause is enough to show that one of these exceptions applies, it doesn't show that the person -- a tip does not show that the person has actually committed an offense.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: No, it's not a -- it's not a tip. It's a call from a French police official.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: So -- so, if it's a call from French police officials, you could take that as a basis, I would think, for -- wholly apart from the immigration laws, for arresting the person and potentially extraditing them to France on that basis. That, I think, is a different question than what's presented by -- by this case or by these exceptions here.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Can I ask you a question about the -- I'm sorry, Mr. Chief Justice. Were you finished?

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: No, I'm done.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay. About the burden of proof, I thought your argument depended on it being clear and convincing evidence. Why would you say that that hasn't been briefed? I might not be following -- I mean, I know that Mr. Joshi is saying, well, for parole, there's a satisfied standard and then we defer the admissibility determination and that is by clear and convincing proof in the context of the removal proceeding. But I thought that you did have a position that it's clear and convincing at the border.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: So what our argument depends on, because of the way in which this case has been teed up from the Second Circuit on, is that there is a determination that has to be made at the border.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Mm-hmm.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: The government's position in the Second Circuit was we have no burden at the border. Our only burden is in the ultimate removal hearing. So our position, again, the way this has been teed up, is yes, you do have a determination -- an obligation to make a determination at the border. Now the government has never challenged, never disputed that if a standard or until the -- until the end of this case, has not disputed that if a burden applies on it at the border, that that border -- that that burden is clear and convincing evidence. Now, if we were to brief and argue what --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I guess I don't really understand Mr. Joshi to have said this. And he might be right or he might be wrong. But, as I understand it, he says there is a burden at the border or maybe he says this was like part of his 1, 2, 3, answer. One is that there's really no burden at the border, but two was there's like a, you know, are you reasonably satisfied burden at the border.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: That is what he says today. The point is that 2 and 3 in his three-part answer are new as of today and the merits reply.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: And -- and why is that? That, you know, maybe I haven't focused enough on this, but when I'm -- I'm staring at the QP in the -- in the first brief, and it says whether to remove as inadmissible an LPR, blah, blah, blah, whether to remove that person, the government must prove that it possessed clear and convincing evidence that the alien committed the offense at the time of his reentry. So why doesn't that set up the question that you're saying was not involved?

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: I take your point about the question presented, Justice Kagan. That's precisely why in the BIO we said: Wait a minute, it looks like your argument might depend on the Court deciding not only whether there is a burden at the border but what that burden is. And this case would be a poor vehicle for it because you didn't raise that issue of what the burden would be in the Second Circuit. Cert reply, page 11, what they then came back with and said is "to confirm, the government is not challenging the clear and convincing standard. This question is about when the government must carry that burden. Because the Second Circuit created a circuit conflict and disrupted immigration enforcement rather than" -- sorry, "disrupted immigration enforcement by incorrectly requiring the government to carry that burden at the border, this Court's review is" -- "is warranted." The whole case was teed up on the assumption that if a standard applies at the burden, it's clear and convincing, but their argument was there is no burden at the border at all.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: But that's because they thought that they could parole at the border. I think, if we accept your argument that the admissibility determination has to be made at the border, then it necessarily means that the clear and convincing standard applies, as the government has teed up this case. I agree, for purposes of this case, they said, as for the admissibility determination, clear and convincing is the standard. But, if you win, it's necessarily saying that the clear and convincing determination applies at the border. I might not be tracking.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: I -- I don't think that's quite right, Justice Barrett.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: If -- if we win, all the Court needs to hold is that contrary to the government's initial argument and arguments to the Second Circuit, Judge Sullivan was correct that there is a burden at the border. The government does need to show at the -- as of that time at the border the returning LPR was subject to one of the exceptions. The Court could --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: So the government can parole -- you're conceding then that the government can parole in Mr. Joshi's kind of 1, 2, 3 formulation that he can parole, the officer can do so at the border, but you're just saying, well, maybe it's not clear and convincing?

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: No. He -- he can parole at the border.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Mm-hmm.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: Our argument would be -- again, if it were briefed or in a -- in a future case, our argument would be that the correct standard is clear and convincing evidence. But the government would be free to argue that it's something less. The government at that point would be free to argue, and -- and the BIA could have the first crack at this, to say, okay, we accept the Court's holding that there is a burden at the border; we think that's less than clear and convincing.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: I am so confused, though. I thought that your argument was that they had to make the determination whether one of the exceptions applied at the border, which goes --

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: -- to whether they were seeking admission, and that that standard was clear and convincing. And if they have to make the admissibility, like, whether one of these exceptions applies, so whether the LPR counts as someone seeking admission or not, why wouldn't -- if the admission -- if that -- if that determination has to be made at the border, why wouldn't the clear and convincing standard apply on your theory of the case?

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: Again, I think it would. Under Mr. Joshi's theory of the case, they seem to now be acknowledging that there is a determination that has to be made at the border. But he says perhaps that standard is just determination to the satisfaction of the officer, or maybe it's the Third Circuit's standard at most, determination supported by probable cause. The question of what standard should apply at the border is something that the BIA could explore in another case, the lower courts could explore in the first instance, but it's not something that has been briefed or fleshed out here, other than in today's argument and in passing in the reply brief.

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: And that's because, Mr. Dvoretzky -- I'm just trying to make sure I understand as well. I was, with Justice Barrett, a little confused. So it's because the government's initial argument was that we don't have to have the border agent determining whether or not the person is in any of those buckets, they can just parole the person in.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: That was their original thing, that we -- you don't have to -- and now the government is saying we admit that the person has to be seeking admission and, therefore, one of these five have to apply, but now they are arguing the standard for determining whether or not one of those applies is something less than clear and convincing.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: Right. Their -- their argument was parole first --

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: -- figure out whether parole was proper later. Judge Sullivan, for the Second Circuit, correctly rejected that argument because that is not the sequence that the INA contemplates.

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: And you understand them to today be agreeing that that's not the sequence. We need to have a determination -- some determination made. And now the dispute is over what level of proof is necessary --

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: -- to make that determination.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: They -- they just think, here today and in passing in the reply brief, that that standard of proof is just to the officer's satisfaction. Or maybe it's probable cause. We would say it's clear and convincing evidence. None of that has been briefed or developed. Those should be issues for another case after this Court either affirms on the narrow timing question or DIGs. And, look, I'm not going to be the lawyer who stands here and tell -- tells you what the Court will or won't do in an April argument, but I think this would be an -- an appropriate case in which to DIG --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: You should have been here yesterday.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: An appropriate case in which to DIG rather than take on these questions.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Just to be clear, we will take appropriate action without regard to --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: -- without regard to the calendar. Justice Thomas? Justice Alito?

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: I am a bit baffled by what you're saying about what question is before us. I read the question presented the way Justice Kagan did. The government -- the question presented says the question is whether -- "whether the government must prove that it possessed clear and convincing evidence of the offense at the time of the LPR's last reentry into the United States." And the passage that you read from on page 11 of the cert reply just says "the government is not challenging the clear and convincing standard. The case is about when the government must carry that burden." So I interpreted that to mean they admit, at least for the purposes of this case, that clear and convincing is the standard at the removal proceeding. But they never -- but the -- the question of what is the standard at the time when the alien is trying to come back into the United States is something different.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: I -- I think it is something different, but as I read page 11, I think what that is saying is the case is about when the government must carry the clear and convincing evidence standard. It's not about whether there is some other burden or standard that the government could conceivably have to satisfy instead. It's do we carry clear and convincing evidence at time 1 or do we carry clear and convincing evidence at time 2? That's the premise on which the government brought this case to the Court.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Yeah. And you're saying they have to do it at Time 1.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: We're saying that it has to be at Time 1 because they haven't disputed that, if there is a burden, that is the burden. If they want to dispute that, they are free to do so in another case where they present that to the lower courts, perhaps with BIA guidance to go on.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Okay. Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: That's the point, which is they've never said to us, except here, this is our burden at the border.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: Correct. And -- and in -- in brief passing in the reply brief for the first time.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: They've suggested there's no burden at the border --

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- essentially.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Unreviewable burden.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: That's right, Justice Sotomayor.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan? Justice Gorsuch?

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: I had thought the case -- and maybe I'm -- you can correct me, but I -- I thought the case was about whether the government could satisfy its burden in a removal proceeding by clear and convincing evidence showing at that time the facts without regard to what happened in a separate parole proceeding at the border.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: I -- I wouldn't think of it as a separate parole proceeding at the border.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: I -- I think what the case is about is whether --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: It's a removal proceeding, right?

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: That's what's before us. And the question is, does the government have to prove its burden with respect to whether the immigrant committed the crime by clear and convincing evidence at time 1 or time 2? Right?

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: But -- but, Justice Gorsuch, the only way that we ever got to that removal proceeding --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, I -- I understand.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: -- was -- was because

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: -- because there was a parole proceeding that preceded it.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: I understand that. Yeah. Okay. Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kavanaugh? Justice Barrett? Justice Jackson? Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: SHAY DVORETZKY
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Rebuttal, Mr. Joshi?

turns.name: SOPAN JOSHI
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice. Just four quick points. First, on Respondent's new request for a DIG, I think we've read the -- we've had the QP read aloud a couple of times. We clearly were challenging the requirement that there be a clear and convincing evidentiary requirement at the border. The Second Circuit said yes. We say no. That's all you need to say. That's fairly encompassed in the question presented. As my response to Justice Jackson said, we don't think there is any evidentiary requirement at the border. We think that the government can satisfy its burden, as Justice Gorsuch just said, in the removal proceeding by proving that the -- that the Respondent was, in fact, seeking an admission at the time. My -- you know, my -- my friend says that our original position was that we can, you know, parole someone to defer a decision on whether parole is possible and we've only recently switched tracks. We have never said that. He has not quoted a single line from any brief below or here in which we said that. And, in fact, I'll just -- sorry to do this -- read to you from our opening brief in this Court on page 32: "Observing that the INA authorizes the Secretary to parole any alien applying for admission, respondent has argued that 'the government must determine whether an LPR is treated as an applicant for admission before the non-citizen is paroled into the country.' True enough." We never disputed that. We just said that obligation in no way implies that in subsequent removal proceedings, the government must prove that it made the correct determination by offering only the evidence that it had in hand at that prior time. That's what the Second Circuit held. That holding is clearly wrong. And the circuit split it created is intolerable right now. I mean, JFK is in one circuit. Newark is in another. Houston Hobby -- or Houston International is in a third. Each of these three circuits has a different rule. And it's going to be incredibly hard to manage if you DIG this case. And if you affirm, all of the consequences that Justice Barrett and -- and others have been discussing are going to hold. It's -- we're basically going to find that the "seeking admission" clause is -- is unenforceable at the border. That leads me to my second point, which is Mr. Dvoretzky basically said you're going to need a conviction or just an admission of guilt. I don't see how that's consistent with the INA's text. It says "committed an offense." It's certainly not consistent with Romanette (iii) about criminal activity abroad. It's not even consistent with offenses identified in 1182(a)(2) because most of them, as I said, don't require a conviction to be inadmissible, the -- the human trafficking, the drug trafficking, the prostitution, some of the other ones. So you're going to need a conviction just to determine whether you've committed an offense, but then you don't need the conviction to render you inadmissible. That's completely topsy-turvy. That can't possibly be what Congress wanted. And that's to say nothing as I said of the other five clauses. If it's clear and convincing, even something as straightforward as Romanette (ii), I think, is if you've been absent for 180 days from the country, and suppose a border officer says, well, I see your passport stamp and your round trip ticket was 200 days ago so you've been absent for 180 days, and the LPR says oh, no, I haven't, although that's my round trip ticket, in the interim, I have been driving back and forth into the country several times so I don't have 180 days of absence. Does the officer have clear and convincing evidence that the LPR is not telling the truth? I can't imagine that he does. But that's exactly the sort of thing where the officer might say all right, I'm going to parole you and we're going to figure this out. In 30 days show up and show me that you've been driving back and forth across the border where we don't stamp your passport, and if necessary, we'll go to removal proceedings and then the IJ will decide on the stand whether he's credible or not. Under Respondent's view, even if that LPR were not telling the truth, too bad because the border officer in the moment didn't have clear and convincing evidence of this untruthful assertion. That, again, cannot possibly be right. Finally, in terms of the -- the statement that my friend made that there's just no meaningful check on the ability to parole LPRs, as I said, Number 1, there is the presumption of regularity. And you would really have to have a DHS-wide conspiracy for the parade of horribles that Respondent talks about to materialize. It is telling that our rule has been the background rule for decades, if not over a century. It certainly has expressly been the rule in this circumstance in the Fifth Circuit for more than a decade. You haven't seen these consequences. The Third Circuit, since 2011, probable cause but still much lower than clear and convincing. You haven't seen these circumstances. So I just don't think it's reasonable to say that. Congress provided that parole decisions are unreviewable. It made a judgment that they're unreviewable. And I know that's always going to cause some heartburn and difficulty when you say something's unreviewable but unless you think all of DHS is composed of, to use a phrase, monsters or idiots, that's just the system that Congress enacted.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
